does: This paper introduces Bounded Parameter-Efficient Unlearning (BPEU), a method for machine unlearning in foundation models that addresses instability in existing approaches. The core contribution is applying bounded activation functions to LoRA adapters in transformer MLP layers during unlearning, specifically using gradient ascent on forgotten data paired with gradient descent on retained data. The authors provide theoretical analysis showing how standard gradient ascent destabilizes weight dynamics in MLP layers, causing unbounded growth that degrades both forgetting quality and model retention. They propose GD+Sine, which uses sine-bounded MLP adapters to control weight evolution during the ascent phase. Experiments on Vision Transformer class deletion using CIFAR-100 demonstrate that GD+Sine achieves superior performance across multiple architectures (ViT-B/16, ViT-L/14, DeiT-S), maintaining high forget quality while preserving model utility compared to existing unlearning methods.
connects: This work builds on the gradient difference method for machine unlearning, which has become a standard approach but suffers from instability issues when combined with cross-entropy loss. The paper extends prior research on parameter-efficient fine-tuning, particularly LoRA-based adapters, by applying these techniques to the unlearning setting. It responds to the challenge of stable optimization in transformer-based models, connecting to broader work on understanding transformer training dynamics and weight stability. The theoretical framework analyzing MLP layer behavior during gradient ascent builds on mechanistic understanding of transformer architectures. The paper also relates to concurrent efforts in privacy-preserving machine learning and safe foundation model deployment, addressing the practical need for reliable unlearning as models are deployed in real-world settings where data removal requests must be handled reliably.
enables: This work enables more reliable privacy-preserving deployment of foundation models by providing a stable unlearning mechanism suitable for large-scale systems. The bounded function approach generalizes beyond MLP layers, suggesting future applications to other transformer components or architectural variants. The theoretical framework explaining destabilization during gradient ascent could guide the development of alternative stabilization techniques and inform design choices in other optimization contexts. The method's parameter-efficiency makes unlearning more practical for large models, potentially enabling regular retraining or fine-tuning cycles that incorporate new privacy requirements. Future work could explore applications to other types of unlearning scenarios beyond class deletion, such as instance-level or concept-level forgetting, and extend the approach to other foundation model families like large language models where similar optimization challenges likely occur.
